No AI or agent strategy. Siloed experimentation without coordination and no clear link between AI initiatives and business goals. Leadership has not defined what an agent-first organization means. User experiences with agents are unplanned, with significant friction and inconsistency.

Begin documenting early hypotheses for how AI agents could create value. Identify 2–3 high-impact use cases for initial focus. Establish leadership sponsorship and basic governance. Gather user feedback and identify experience gaps.

An emerging AI vision is articulated but sits outside core enterprise strategy, resulting in parallel planning and limited ability to scale beyond pilots. AI agents are explored in isolated scenarios with limited connection to business priorities. Basic agent experiences exist but users encounter friction and inconsistency.

Formalize the AI strategy by documenting target outcomes, priority use-case themes, and guiding principles. Facilitate cross-functional discussions to align business and IT. Create user onboarding and training programs. Secure leadership approval and communicate the strategy broadly.

A formal, documented AI strategy is in place and aligned to business objectives. Leadership sponsorship is explicit and a roadmap exists for agent adoption across functions. The organization has begun defining what agent-first means for key workflows. Users can easily discover and use agents relevant to their work with consistent interaction patterns.

Embed the AI and agent strategy into enterprise planning and budgeting cycles. Scale successful patterns across business units. Implement sophisticated user personalization. Refine success metrics so AI outcomes are clearly linked to business performance.

Enterprise and IT strategy are fully aligned around an agent-first operating model. Business units have aligned sub-plans coordinated through a Center of Excellence. Leadership actively sponsors execution and reviews outcomes. Sophisticated user experiences adapt to individual needs through unified interfaces.

Introduce continuous strategy review processes to incorporate learnings, market changes, and new AI capabilities. Use adoption and value signals to refine priorities. Extend the strategy across multiyear horizons and develop next-generation interaction paradigms.

The organization operates as an agent-first enterprise with a living, adaptive strategy. C-suite accountability for AI strategy execution. AI strategy is dynamic and continuously refreshed based on real-world outcomes, telemetry, and external signals. Users interact with AI agents as naturally as with human colleagues.

Maintain strategic excellence through continuous improvement and industry leadership. Invest in thought leadership, external benchmarking, and ecosystem partnerships. Pioneer new user experience paradigms for AI interaction.

Business processes are designed for human-only execution with no workflow redesign for agents. No formal tracking of outcomes or benefits from AI agents. Success is described anecdotally with no defined baselines, success criteria, or linkage between agents and business outcomes.

Map one high-impact process end-to-end and identify agent interaction points. Define basic value dimensions such as productivity, quality, cost, or experience. Establish baseline measurements and begin capturing before-and-after data for active initiatives.

Limited use of agents to support some steps of tasks with incremental improvements to existing processes. No coordinated end-to-end transformation. Early qualitative value recognition exists but measurement is inconsistent across projects. ROI calculations vary by team and value is not tied to enterprise KPIs.

Facilitate cross-functional, AI-first process design workshops. Reimagine workflows from the ground up rather than optimizing individual steps. Introduce a standard value measurement template for all agents. Begin aggregating results across initiatives.

End-to-end workflows are redesigned so agents participate in execution, not just assistance. Human-agent roles are documented and redesigned processes are tracked against measurable business outcomes. Agents have defined KPIs and business cases include expected ROI. Value insights inform prioritization but remain siloed by domain.

Extend redesign to cross-functional and cross-system processes. Standardize process transformation playbooks and reusable orchestration patterns. Roll up agent KPIs into a central value dashboard aligned with business OKRs and leadership reporting.

Agents orchestrate multi-step, cross-system workflows with human-led, agent-operated processes as standard practice. Business units proactively propose transformation initiatives. Regular value reporting to leadership with proven ROI across multiple agents. Under-performing agents are reviewed, redesigned, or retired.

Enable autonomous agent decision-making in low-risk scenarios. Implement real-time process optimization. Automate value tracking into centralized dashboards with comparative analysis to identify highest-impact agent patterns.

Core business processes are agent-operated, adaptive, and data-driven. Agents initiate, execute, and adapt processes in real time, handling exceptions and learning from outcomes. Enterprise-wide real-time view of AI value with fully data-driven decisions. The organization confidently articulates total AI value internally and externally.

Maintain excellence through continuous process innovation and a unified AI operating model. Continuously evolve metrics to capture emerging value such as new business models, cultural shift, and innovation velocity. Share best practices across industry.

No AI-specific governance, security, or operational support for agents. Agents operate without formal oversight, risk assessment, or compliance checks. All agents treated the same regardless of purpose or risk. No clarity on ownership, accountability, or decision rights. Problems are discovered informally with no structured feedback loop.

Establish minimum guardrails and define who can create, publish, and share agents. Introduce basic AI awareness across IT, security, and compliance. Assign clear ownership for each agent. Implement basic logging and usage tracking. Establish feedback channels for reporting problems.

Basic controls and policies are documented but inconsistently applied. Some guidelines and approval steps exist. Governance is largely reactive and dependent on individual diligence. Basic monitoring exists using out-of-the-box platform reports. Support is reactive with informal runbooks and unclear accountability across teams.

Publish an organizational baseline for identity, access, data governance, and compliance controls. Formalize a governance framework with roles, review checkpoints, and compliance requirements. Begin classifying agents by intended use and blast radius. Establish basic incident handling and escalation paths.

Security, governance, and compliance practices for AI are documented and enforced. Agents classified by purpose, criticality, and autonomy level with a zoned governance model. Central agent registry and audit logging established. Mission-critical agents have defined SLAs, monitoring, and escalation. Incident management processes are documented and followed.

Automate governance where possible including environment provisioning and policy enforcement. Scale governance through federation by delegating low-risk approvals to teams within guardrails. Standardize operational runbooks by agent classification. Schedule regular performance and quality reviews.

Governance is risk-based and partially automated with a cross-functional AI Council. Federated model with central standards and delegated approvals for low-risk agents. Operations are proactive with anomaly detection, automated remediation, and regular operational reporting. Continuous monitoring and policy-driven compliance integrated into operations.

Expand automation to approvals, monitoring, and compliance reporting. Use analytics to identify emerging risks and continuously update governance policies. Introduce KPI-based governance with incident, reliability, and trust signals. Expand automation to predictive maintenance and self-healing.

Agents treated as tiered digital services with differentiated SLAs, controls, and autonomy levels. Governance continuously adapts based on usage, risk, and regulation. Operations are predictive and self-optimizing with many problems detected and resolved automatically. Practices continuously evolve with new agent capabilities and regulations.

Maintain maturity through continuous adaptation. Stay ahead of emerging threats, regulatory changes, and new agent patterns. Pioneer new governance and operational patterns. Share best practices across industry and partners.

Agent work is exploratory and fragmented. Teams experiment without a defined technology plan. Data access is ad hoc with no consistent retrieval strategy. No clarity on when to use SaaS agents versus custom builds and no shared data foundation. Solutions are fragile, undocumented, and difficult to reuse or scale.

Define an initial technology plan and standardize on approved platforms. Inventory where relevant data lives and document connectivity gaps. Establish basic platform selection criteria based on use-case complexity.

Teams begin converging on platforms but choices are inconsistent and driven by team preference rather than use-case fit. Data is partially prepared but structured business data remains siloed with limited connectors. ALM and telemetry are inconsistent. Patterns are driven by individual team experience rather than enterprise guidance.

Standardize platform selection based on scope and complexity. Define reference architectures and integration patterns. Establish standard solution templates, approved connectors, and basic CI/CD pipelines to promote consistency and reduce rework.

A documented technology plan exists with clear distinctions between SaaS agents, Copilot Studio agents, and advanced build paths. Data architecture follows a clear pattern with unified business data organized for validated agent grounding. Standard platforms, reference architectures, and integration patterns are defined and reused. ALM practices are established.

Strengthen scalability, security, and observability. Embed monitoring, logging, and evaluation into the standard architecture. Validate single-agent versus multi-agent needs through structured experiments. Expand and certify datasets for high-value domains.

Technology and data foundations are enterprise-grade with clear visibility into systems, APIs, and data across workflows. Secure-by-design access patterns, centralized telemetry, and automated evaluations are standard. Deployments are automated and reliable with centralized monitoring across the organization.

Shift from central execution to federated delivery. Embed guardrails into platforms and expand self-service safely. Evolve standards to support higher autonomy and new agent patterns. Evaluate new capabilities and incorporate them where they add value.

Technology and data foundations continuously evolve based on telemetry and emerging patterns. Federated teams build independently while guardrails enforce quality by default. Architecture supports complex multi-agent scenarios and federated development at scale. Continuous improvement of technical standards is embedded into the operating model.
Maintain excellence through ongoing architectural review, experimentation, and knowledge sharing. Invest in platform evolution to stay ahead of emerging AI patterns and requirements.

AI adoption exists only in isolated experiments or pilots. No shared enterprise narrative for AI and leadership sponsorship is weak or absent. Agents are seen as optional, with no clear ownership for adoption, value, or risk. Employees lack clarity on expectations and learning is informal and self-directed.

Establish visible executive sponsorship and articulate a shared agent-first narrative. Encourage early exploration and share wins to seed interest. Begin identifying potential champions. Introduce basic Responsible AI awareness and principles.

Interest is growing but adoption depends on motivated individuals or teams. Training and communities exist sporadically without a structured program or operating model. Decision rights and ownership are unclear, leading to inconsistent practices and friction.

Define and document a baseline AI operating model including roles, ownership, intake, and escalation paths. Introduce consistent communications and structured enablement. Formalize early upskilling programs. Create a dedicated internal community space and begin recognizing contributors.

A documented organizational approach exists with a Center of Excellence providing standards and enablement while execution is federated. Structured onboarding, learning paths, and community events are in place. Knowledge repositories and regular showcases emerge. Leadership endorsement is present but uneven.

Embed the operating model into planning, funding, and governance cycles. Operationalize decision rights and introduce adoption and confidence metrics. Expand champion networks across departments. Reinforce behaviors through recognition, storytelling, and leadership forums.

Agent-assisted work is standard practice across functions. Incentives and performance expectations reinforce responsible agent use. Culture supports experimentation within clear ethical guardrails and business units proactively propose agent-enabled improvements. Communities are active and self-driven.

Optimize ways of working and incentives toward outcomes and responsible autonomy. Use adoption, sentiment, and feedback data to refine norms. Develop advanced Responsible AI competencies across the organization. Prepare for greater agent autonomy with clear ethical decision boundaries.

The organization operates as an agent-first enterprise. Grassroots ideas are rapidly surfaced, governed, and scaled with embedded ethical considerations. Culture, leadership, incentives, and learning are fully aligned around responsible AI practices. Communities actively reinforce standards and innovation.

Maintain maturity through continuous evolution of the operating model as agent capabilities mature. Sustain momentum through recognition, advanced challenges, and external community engagement. Lead industry discussions around Responsible AI culture.
